metadata.title: Quick-fire: Asymmetric reciprocity
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-reciprocity-001 (basic trust-vs-vigilance) and qf-reciprocity-003 (pattern-vs-moment tension). This round probes situations where direct symmetric reciprocity isn't possible: helping in advance, power-asymmetric help, stranger-favor reciprocation, formal-vs-informal arrangements. Tests whether the user's cooperation/reciprocity instincts hold up when the ledger metaphor breaks down.
metadata.domain_anchor: Mauss 1925 The Gift on non-equivalent reciprocity; Sahlins 1972 on generalized vs balanced reciprocity; Cropanzano & Mitchell social-exchange theory for power-asymmetric contexts.

items.prompt: You see a colleague struggling on a project that's not connected to yours. They haven't asked for help. You have an hour you could give.

options.text: Offer help unprompted — generosity in advance, no expectation of return.
Wait until they ask. Unrequested help can be patronizing, and you can't really know what they need without being told.

items.prompt: A wealthy friend has been generous to you over the years (treating you to meals, picking up checks, lending things freely). They now ask for a favor that's modest for them but moderately costly for you.

options.text: Do it. The asymmetric giving over the years is real even if neither of you keeps a ledger.
Decline if it doesn't work for you. Their generosity was their choice; it doesn't create an obligation that costs you.

items.prompt: You're in a senior position. A junior colleague needs substantial help on something. The asymmetry means they can't reciprocate directly to you in any near future.

options.text: Help generously. Pay-it-forward toward the field is the way the asymmetric reciprocity actually closes.
Help, but make the expectations clear: a specific scope, not unlimited. Even pay-it-forward requires structure.

items.prompt: A stranger does you a real favor — returns your wallet with cash intact, helps in a medical situation, lets you in a critical line. The interaction will be one-shot; you'll never see them again.

options.text: Press a meaningful gesture on them — a substantial tip, a follow-up gift sent later, an explicit thank-you note.
Thank them warmly in the moment. Pay it forward to the next stranger you encounter who needs something.

items.prompt: You and a friend have a written, informal agreement (shared business expense, joint subscription, splitting a recurring cost). They want to switch from the written terms to 'we'll just figure it out.'

options.text: Agree. The friendship is closer than the agreement; written terms can be more about distrust than fairness.
Keep the written terms. Friction now is better than the larger frictions that often follow informal arrangements.

items.prompt: A friend who's been generous to you in the past is going through a hard time. You'd like to help substantially. They explicitly don't want it framed as 'paying back' — they say their past help was unconditional.

options.text: Help anyway, and respect the framing — don't reference the past. The help is generosity in the present.
Help but quietly hold the ledger acknowledgment in your own head. They get the help they need; you get to honor the actual history.
